title: The churro stand at eight
summary: An Andalusian churro seller and her daughter argue about closing time.

body.speaker: Carmen
body.text: Mama, it is already eight. We can close. Nobody is coming.

body.speaker: Pilar
body.text: We close at half past eight. Always half past eight. For thirty-one years, half past eight.

body.speaker: Carmen
body.text: But look. The street is empty. We have six churros left. Let's go home.

body.speaker: Pilar
body.text: Six churros is six customers we have not met yet. Sit down.

body.speaker: Carmen
body.text: [Carmen sits down on the small stool.] Mama, your back hurts. I can see it.

body.speaker: Pilar
body.text: My back hurts at five, when I make the dough. My back is fine at eight. The last half hour brings different customers. They are the lonely ones. The man who works late. The taxi driver. The mother with a sick baby who needs sugar. They cannot come earlier. They need us to wait.

body.speaker: Carmen
body.text: I have never thought about it that way.

body.speaker: Pilar
body.text: When you take this stand from me, you will think about it that way. Or you will close at eight and the right people will go home with nothing in their hands. Your choice.

body.speaker: Carmen
body.text: Has someone really come at twenty-five past eight?

body.speaker: Pilar
body.text: Last Thursday. A young man. He was crying. He bought one churro. He did not eat it. He held it. He thanked me three times. He went away. I do not know his story. But I know he could not have come earlier.

body.speaker: Carmen
body.text: [Carmen is quiet.] Mama, when I take the stand, I will close at half past eight.

body.speaker: Pilar
body.text: Good. Now stop talking. Someone is walking up the street.

annotations.meaningVi: để tiếp tục / để cầm cự
nhận quầy này từ tôi — ý: kế thừa công việc

annotations.phrase: I do not know his story
annotations.meaningVi: tôi không biết chuyện của anh ta
đi lên (theo) phố — đang đi tới

grammarNotes.title: Future with 'will' for promises and predictions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng 'will + V (nguyên thể)' để hứa, dự đoán hoặc nói về quyết định. Trong bài: 'When I take the stand, I will close at half past eight.' (lời hứa của Carmen). 'When you take this stand from me, you will think about it that way.' (dự đoán của Pilar). Lưu ý 'when' giống 'if' — trong mệnh đề 'when' tương lai, vẫn dùng hiện tại đơn ('When I take', không phải 'When I will take').
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 uses 'will + base verb' for promises, predictions and on-the-spot decisions. From the dialogue: 'When I take the stand, I will close at half past eight.' (Carmen's promise). 'When you take this stand from me, you will think about it that way.' (Pilar's prediction). Like 'if', a future 'when' clause uses the present simple, not 'will' ('When I take', not 'When I will take').

translationVi:
Carmen: Mẹ ơi, tám giờ rồi. Mình đóng được rồi. Không ai đến nữa.

Pilar: Mình đóng lúc tám rưỡi. Luôn luôn tám rưỡi. Ba mươi mốt năm rồi, tám rưỡi.

Carmen: Nhưng nhìn này. Phố vắng. Còn sáu cái churro. Đi về thôi.

Pilar: Sáu cái churro là sáu khách mình chưa gặp. Ngồi xuống.

Carmen: [Carmen ngồi xuống ghế đẩu.] Mẹ ơi, lưng mẹ đau. Con thấy mà.

Pilar: Lưng mẹ đau lúc năm giờ sáng, khi nhào bột. Tám giờ thì lưng mẹ ổn. Khách trong nửa giờ cuối khác với khách lúc sáu giờ. Họ là những người cô đơn. Anh đi làm khuya. Bác tài taxi. Bà mẹ có em bé ốm cần ít đường để cầm cự. Họ không thể đến sớm hơn. Họ cần mình đợi.

Carmen: Con chưa bao giờ nghĩ vậy.

Pilar: Khi nào con nhận quầy này, con sẽ nghĩ vậy. Hoặc con đóng cửa lúc tám giờ và những người cần sẽ về tay không. Tuỳ con.

Carmen: Đã có ai đến lúc tám giờ hai mươi lăm thật chưa?

Pilar: Thứ Năm tuần trước. Một anh thanh niên. Anh ấy đang khóc. Anh mua một cái churro. Anh không ăn. Anh chỉ cầm. Anh cảm ơn mẹ ba lần. Anh đi. Mẹ không biết chuyện của anh. Nhưng mẹ biết anh không thể đến sớm hơn.

Carmen: [Carmen im lặng.] Mẹ ơi, khi con nhận quầy, con sẽ đóng lúc tám rưỡi.

Pilar: Tốt. Giờ thôi nói. Có người đang đi tới đằng phố kia.

questions.prompt: What does Carmen want to do at the start?
What time does Pilar always close?
How long has she done this?
How does Pilar describe the six remaining churros?
options: 'Six churros is six customers we have not met yet.'
At five in the morning, when she makes the dough.
Only at eight in the evening.
questions.explanation: 'My back hurts at five in the morning.'

questions.prompt: Who are the late customers, according to Pilar?
options: The lonely ones — workers who work late, taxi drivers, a mother with a sick baby.

questions.prompt: What does Pilar say will happen if Carmen closes at eight?
options: The right people will go home with nothing in their hands.

questions.prompt: What did the young man last Thursday do with his churro?
options: He held it without eating it, thanked Pilar three times, and left.
When she takes the stand, she will close at half past eight.
She will close at eight from now on.
She will quit the family business.
She will open at four instead.
Pilar says someone is walking up the street.
A small business rule that looks inefficient is actually an act of care for the people who can only come at the end of the day.

cloze.template:
Carmen: Mama, it is already eight. We can {{b1}}. Nobody is coming.
Pilar: We close at half past eight. Always half past eight. For thirty-one {{b2}}, half past eight.
Carmen: But look. The street is empty. We have six churros left. Let's go home.
Pilar: Six churros is six {{b3}} we have not met yet. Sit down.
Carmen: Mama, your back {{b4}}. I can see it.
Pilar: My back hurts at five, when I make the {{b5}}. My back is fine at eight. The last half hour brings different customers. They are the {{b6}} ones. The man who works late. The taxi driver. The mother with a sick baby who needs sugar. They cannot come earlier. They need us to wait.
Carmen: I have never thought about it that way.
Pilar: When you take this stand from me, you {{b7}} think about it that way. Or you will close at eight and the right people will go home with nothing in their hands. Your choice.
Carmen: Has someone really come at twenty-five past eight?
Pilar: Last {{b8}}. A young man. He was crying. He bought one churro. He did not eat it. He held it. He thanked me three times. He went away. I do not know his story. But I know he could not have come earlier.
Carmen: Mama, when I take the stand, I will close at {{b9}} past eight.
Pilar: Good. Now stop talking. Someone is walking up the {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: Six customers we have not met.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Pilar refuses to close thirty minutes earlier because of customers she cannot predict. When have you been tempted to optimise away a small piece of slack that was actually quietly serving someone?
